lens.included_activities: US other-management-consulting firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that repeatedly provide outsourced advice, analysis, recommendations, facilitation, and implementation support to external organizations, including telecommunications and utilities management consulting.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal strategy units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, administrative and general management consulting, human-resources consulting, marketing consulting, process and logistics consulting, and practices whose client relationships, expertise, or delivery capability cannot transfer beyond an individual founder.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, nonprofit, and government clients buy scoped advisory projects, fixed-fee deliverables, time-and-materials work, value-priced engagements, and recurring retainers; repeat revenue arises from continuing client relationships, successive initiatives, and implementation support.

a.bridge: BLS reports an occupation mix for the broader NAICS 541600 consulting sector, and a randomized field experiment measures performance on selected BCG consulting tasks; neither measures the wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated tasks in NAICS 541618. I map exposure across research, synthesis, interview and meeting notes, data analysis, benchmarking, first-draft recommendations, proposals, reports, presentations, project administration, and knowledge retrieval, while retaining human time for problem framing, expert diagnosis, stakeholder trust, facilitation, political judgment, field observation, and implementation. The BCG task set can overstate exposure because it preselected AI-suitable exercises, while broader occupation data can understate it by including scientific consulting roles outside this code.
a.rationale: Consulting is concentrated in highly paid knowledge work: in broader NAICS 541600, management analysts were 11.91% of employment, management occupations 16.21%, project-management specialists 3.55%, computer occupations 9.56%, and office support 14.98% in 2023. In a 758-consultant BCG experiment, AI users completed inside-frontier tasks over 25% faster and completed over 12% more tasks. A 48% base recognizes broad exposure in analysis and production without treating the whole advisory relationship as substitutable.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study of NAICS 541618 firms reporting hours and compensation by research, modeling, interviews, analysis, deliverable production, sales, facilitation, implementation, and administration before and after AI use would materially change a.
caveats: No source directly reports a wage-weighted task-exposure share for this six-digit residual industry.
The OEWS mix covers management, scientific, and technical consulting broadly and likely differs materially from 541618.

The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio is 1.3991, which is economically implausible as a true compensation share and reflects a 2024-wage/2022-receipts mismatch plus harmonization; this materially weakens the labor-opportunity foundation but is not re-estimated here.

rho.bridge: The BCG experiment establishes technical gains under controlled access, while McKinsey surveys organization-wide AI use rather than implementation of exposed labor opportunity in LMM consulting firms. I translate 88% regular organizational AI use, but only about 6% high-performing scaled adopters, into a five-year implementation share that discounts for client confidentiality, weak proprietary data, workflow redesign, quality assurance, employee training, and the uneven AI frontier. Large-enterprise adoption may overstate LMM consulting capability; simpler boutique workflows and low software costs may bias the estimate in the other direction.
rho.rationale: AI is easy to deploy for drafts, research, summarization, and internal knowledge access, and controlled consulting tasks show measurable productivity gains. Yet scaled economic impact remains uncommon, and the BCG experiment found that AI users were 19 percentage points less likely to reach the correct solution on an outside-frontier managerial task. A 57% base assumes substantial five-year implementation with persistent human verification and uneven adoption across specialties.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of US consulting firms with fewer than 250 employees showing AI-active seats, workflow penetration, billable hours saved, rework, client restrictions, failed deployments, and sustained hiring avoidance would materially move rho.
caveats: Tool access and individual speed gains do not prove firm-level operational implementation or avoided hiring.
BCG is far larger and better resourced than the firms in the frozen lens.
The frozen labor ratio's value above one means even a well-researched rho cannot cure uncertainty in the downstream labor base.

e.rationale: The Census definition is inherently an outsourced management-advice service, and consulting-industry evidence indicates high-performing firms can earn 70% of revenue from existing clients. Firms large enough to fall within the frozen EBITDA band should usually have repeat external clients and teams, but eligibility is reduced by founder rainmaking, individual specialist reputation, project-only relationships, customer concentration, and weak codification of intellectual property.
e.change_evidence: A representative CRM and financial-data panel for EBITDA-band NAICS 541618 firms reporting external-client revenue, repeat-client revenue, retainers, top-client concentration, founder-originated sales, team delivery, and post-founder retention would materially change e.
caveats: The frozen n of 256 is already a margin-bridged estimate and is not re-estimated here.
NAICS 541618 is a residual code dominated by telecommunications and utilities management consulting but may include miscoded general consultancies.
Existing-client revenue evidence covers consultants broadly rather than the exact industry and EBITDA band.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among all US employer-business owners, and FOCUS counts announced professional-services transactions rather than qualifying completed transfers among eligible 541618 firms. I anchor to the 22% of employer owners planning to sell or transfer within five years, then exclude gifts and internal reorganizations, uncompleted plans, closures, minority investments, and nontransferable founder practices. Professional-services deal activity supports the upper range, but announced transactions and broad owner intentions likely overstate completed control transfers for this code.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 13% of employer-business owners plan to sell and 11% plan to transfer ownership within five years, with overlaps producing a 22% combined share. The professional-services market recorded 466 announced US transactions in the first nine months of 2025, with consulting and accounting representing more than half. A 14% base reflects active deal and succession pathways after excluding nonqualifying and unrealized outcomes.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or Axial transactions matched to NAICS 541618 and seller EBITDA, plus owner-age, intended-exit, and completed-succession data for consulting boutiques, would materially change s5.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses, not consulting firms or the LMM EBITDA band.
A stated plan does not necessarily close, and some transfers are gifts or internal succession rather than qualifying control sales.
Professional-services deal counts include accounting, staffing, human-capital services, and consulting specialties outside 541618.

q.bridge: A broad consultant survey measures the prevalence of pricing methods, not the discounted five-year share of implemented AI benefit retained by LMM 541618 firms. I treat project and value-based fees as permitting stronger initial capture than hourly billing, retainers as mixed depending on whether hours are capped, and then discount for competitive bids, renewal repricing, client demands for faster delivery, and scope expansion. Solo-consultant pricing evidence may overstate value-pricing flexibility for staffed firms with procurement-led clients, while specialist expertise may support retention above the base.
q.rationale: Consulting Success reports 37% of consultants use project rates, 26% value-based rates, 21% hourly rates, 13% monthly retainers, and 3% performance pricing. That mix lets many firms retain initial productivity gains, but more than 80% had lowered fees to win a client in the 2023 study, indicating bargaining pressure. A 56% base reflects meaningful capture on deliverable- and outcome-priced work followed by five-year sharing through competition and client expectations.
q.change_evidence: Five years of engagement-level proposals, contracts, time records, realized margins, change orders, renewal prices, win/loss data, and client-requested AI discounts from LMM 541618 firms would materially change q.
caveats: The survey population is consultants across niches and likely includes many solo operators below the frozen EBITDA band.
Pricing method prevalence is not revenue-weighted and does not show contract-specific pass-through.
Demand loss belongs in d5 and o, and implementation friction belongs in rho, not in this estimate.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of management analysts across all industries, whereas d5 is constant-price, constant-quality demand for the current outsourced service basket in NAICS 541618. I convert the 9% 2024-34 employment projection to a roughly half-decade demand anchor, recognizing that consulting demand may grow faster through efficiency, cost-control, telecom, utility, and AI-transformation needs or slower because software and client teams produce more work per person. Employment embeds productivity and in-house work, so it may understate output demand but overstate outsourced-firm demand.
d5.rationale: BLS projects management-analyst employment to grow 9% from 2024 to 2034 and expects consulting demand to increase as organizations seek efficiency and cost control. AI adoption itself creates advisory and implementation work, although public-sector cuts, recessions, and self-service analysis can offset it. The base therefore assumes modest five-year real growth with a broad cyclical and substitution range.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of US outsourced telecom, utilities, and other management-consulting spend, deflated by a consulting-services price index and segmented by advisory versus implementation, would materially change d5.
caveats: Management analysts are an occupation across industries and do not measure output of NAICS 541618.
The residual industry has material telecom and utilities exposure not isolated in the occupational projection.
Government procurement, enterprise investment cycles, and AI-driven new-service demand create wide uncertainty.

o.bridge: BLS describes consultant duties and the BCG experiment measures human-plus-AI performance on selected tasks; neither directly measures the year-five share that still requires an accountable consulting-firm operator. I deduct generic research, benchmarking, first-draft analysis, and simple recommendation work that clients can self-serve, while retaining problem framing, access to stakeholders, specialist context, interviews, facilitation, decision accountability, change management, and implementation. Evidence from a top-tier consultancy may overstate the operator role for commodity boutiques, while the outside-frontier failure result supports continued expert oversight.
o.rationale: Management analysts gather information through interviews and observation, analyze organization-specific problems, develop solutions, present recommendations, and confer with managers. These relational and contextual steps are difficult to supply entirely through software. The BCG study's 19-percentage-point accuracy decline outside the AI frontier demonstrates why expert judgment and verification remain valuable, although generic deck-and-report work is vulnerable to self-service. A 78% base preserves a substantial operator role while allowing meaningful elimination.
o.change_evidence: Procurement and client surveys tracking which consulting scopes move in-house or to software, paired with project outcomes, renewals, and human-escalation rates for AI-delivered advisory services, would materially change o.
caveats: Management consulting has no universal licensure requirement or statutory barrier to client self-service.
Operator need varies materially between high-context telecom or utility engagements and generic research or benchmarking.
AI capability boundaries may move rapidly, but persuasive errors can preserve the need for accountable expert review.

narrative.executive_view: Other management consulting has a broad AI-addressable knowledge workflow but a materially unreliable frozen labor input: the supplied compensation-to-receipts ratio exceeds one because of vintage and denominator problems. AI can accelerate research, synthesis, analysis, deliverables, and administration, yet consulting value still depends on problem framing, specialist context, stakeholder trust, facilitation, and implementation. Commercial capture should be better on project and value-based fees than on hourly work, but client bargaining will share gains.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft proposals, interview guides, reports, presentations, summaries, benchmarks, and preliminary analyses; search internal knowledge; and automate project administration. Controlled evidence shows large speed and quality gains on tasks inside the capability frontier. The same evidence shows confident failure outside that frontier, so firms need expert review, task triage, client-data controls, and redesigned quality assurance rather than indiscriminate automation.
narrative.value_capture: Project and value-based engagements can retain an efficiency gain when the promised outcome and fee remain fixed. Hourly work passes through fewer hours, while renewals and competitive procurement should reset some fixed fees. Firms that combine proprietary expertise, implementation responsibility, and measurable client outcomes should retain more than firms selling generic research and presentation production.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen dataset estimates 256 firms in the EBITDA band. Most staffed consulting firms should serve external customers with repeat potential, but founder-dependent sales, concentrated relationships, tacit expertise, and miscoding reduce transferability. Broad small-business succession data and active professional-services M&A support a nontrivial transfer probability, though planned and announced events exceed qualifying completed control transfers.
narrative.demand_durability: Organizations should continue buying help with efficiency, cost control, telecom and utility management, organizational change, and AI adoption. Demand is cyclical and exposed to government and corporate budget cuts, while clients can increasingly self-serve generic analysis. Complex diagnosis, stakeholder alignment, implementation, and responsibility for recommendations support a continuing operator role.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal empirical risk is the frozen labor ratio above one, which can overstate the economic opportunity regardless of primitive quality. Operating risks include hallucinated analysis, confidentiality breaches, loss of junior skill formation, fee compression, scope expansion, public-sector cuts, founder dependence, and clients replacing generic consulting with internal AI. The largest evidence gaps are six-digit occupation mix, task time, net realized AI savings, and completed LMM transfers.
narrative.principal_driver: Research, synthesis, analysis, reporting, presentation, and administrative work are digitally native and demonstrably accelerated by generative AI.
narrative.principal_weakness: The supplied labor ratio is economically implausible at 1.3991, while generic consulting output also faces direct client self-service and fee pressure.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 541618 Other Management Consulting Services
sources.supports: Defines the residual industry as management consulting other than the separately classified administrative/general, HR, marketing, and process/logistics categories, and specifically includes telecommunications and utilities management consulting.

sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Management, Scientific, and Technical Consulting Services
sources.supports: Reports the broader consulting-sector occupation mix, including management occupations at 16.21%, management analysts at 11.91%, project-management specialists at 3.55%, computer occupations at 9.56%, and office and administrative support at 14.98% of employment.

sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Management Analysts
sources.supports: Describes consultant duties and proposal-based work, projects 9% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, and states demand for consulting services should increase as organizations seek efficiency and cost control.

sources.title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of AI on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality
sources.supports: Reports a randomized study of BCG consultants: on 18 inside-frontier tasks AI users completed 12.2% more tasks and worked 25.1% faster with higher performance, while on an outside-frontier task they were 19 percentage points less likely to produce a correct solution.

sources.title: McKinsey Global Survey: The state of AI in 2025
sources.supports: Reports nearly nine in ten respondents' organizations regularly use AI, while about 6% qualify as high performers with significant value and scaled workflow redesign, illustrating wide adoption but limited mature impact.

sources.title: Consulting Success Fees Study 2023
sources.supports: Reports consultant pricing methods: 37% project rate, 26% value-based, 21% hourly, 13% monthly retainer, and 3% performance-based; also reports more than 80% had lowered fees to win a client.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports from 1,264 US owner-workers that within five years 13% of employer owners plan to sell and 11% plan to transfer ownership, with a combined sell/transfer/public share of 22%; 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people 55 or older.

sources.title: FOCUS Professional Services Q3 2025 Report
sources.supports: Reports 466 announced US professional-services M&A transactions in the first nine months of 2025 and says consulting and accounting represented more than half of sector deal volume.
